description: The PSC controls module-level clock gating and reset for all on-chip peripherals. Software programs NEXT state in MDCTL[n], triggers a transition with PTCMD.GO, and polls PTSTAT until completion. MDSTAT[n] reflects the current enable/reset state and isolation. Proper sequencing is required before any peripheral register access to avoid bus errors. This feature enables deterministic bring-up and power management of subsystems.
evidence: DSP Subsystem UG: PSC chapter; Registers MDCTL[n], MDSTAT[n], PTCMD, PTSTAT
dependencies: Main PLL stable; PSC clock; Peripheral power domains
Remarks: Always enable and deassert local resets for target modules via PSC before touching their registers; verify MDSTAT state transitions.

description: The PLL generates system clocks from the reference oscillator and provides programmable multiplication and division. Typical configuration places the PLL in BYPASS and RESET, programs PLLM and PLLDIVn, asserts GO, waits for PLLSTAT lock, and then enables the PLL output. Careful order prevents glitches and ensures downstream modules see valid clocks. Divider updates should follow the documented GO/complete handshake. Stable clocks are prerequisite for DDR and high-speed interfaces.
evidence: Device PLL section: Registers PLLCTL, PLLM, PLLDIVn, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT
dependencies: Reference clock; Power domain active; PSC for downstream modules
Remarks: Apply the safe-by-sequence reprogramming flow; only switch out of BYPASS after lock is confirmed.

description: PINMUX registers select peripheral functions on multiplexed device pins. Software must configure each pin group to the required peripheral function after the module is powered by the PSC. Incorrect muxing can tri-state or conflict signals, preventing interfaces from operating. Configuration should precede peripheral initialization to ensure signals are routed. The data manual enumerates legal mux combinations per pin group.
evidence: Device Configuration module: PINMUXn registers; SPRS371 Signal Descriptions/Pin Multiplexing
dependencies: PSC enable for CFG; Board-level pinouts
Remarks: Centralize pinmux setup early in boot and lock configuration before enabling external drivers.

description: The interrupt mux maps numerous system events to the limited CPU interrupt lines INT4–INT15. Software selects sources in INTMUXx to route EDMA, GPIO, timers, and peripheral events. Proper mapping prevents lost interrupts and ensures ISR prioritization. Combined with the CPU IER/IFR, this forms the core of interrupt control. Static maps should be documented to avoid overlaps during integration.
evidence: Device interrupt selector: INTMUXx registers; SPRU732 CPU interrupt model
dependencies: Peripheral event sources; CPU IER/IFR; Event numbers from data manual
Remarks: Maintain a single owner for INTMUX configuration and reserve lines for latency-critical ISRs.

description: The Event Combiner aggregates many low-priority events onto shared CPU interrupt lines. Software programs ECM mask, flag, and clear registers to control event delivery. This reduces INT line pressure while keeping per-event status visibility. ISRs should read and clear the specific ECM event bits to service sources efficiently. Use ECM for non-latency-critical traffic.
evidence: SPRU732: Event Combiner registers (EVTMASK, EVTFLAG, EVTCLR) and usage
dependencies: INTMUX mapping of ECM outputs; CPU IER/IFR
Remarks: Batch-handle events inside a single ISR and measure service time to size mask/unmask policy.

description: EDMA3 CC manages channels, PaRAM sets, and transfer completion signaling. Programming includes PaRAM OPT/SRC/DST/A_B_CNT, channel maps via DCHMAP/QCHMAP, and event/interrupt control via EER/EESR/IPR/ICR. It supports linking, chaining, and synchronized A/B/C indexing for complex transfers. Interrupts are raised via TCC bits and must be cleared in ICR. Robust initialization clears pending events and errors before enabling traffic.
evidence: EDMA3 UG: CC registers EER/EESR/ESR, IPR/ICR, EMR/EMCR, DCHMAP/QCHMAP; PaRAM layout OPT..CCNT
dependencies: PSC enable; INTMUX for CCINT/TCINT; Memory targets ready
Remarks: Abstract PaRAM programming and channel ownership to avoid conflicts; validate pitches and counts.

description: Transfer Controllers execute programmed transfers and arbitrate through queues. Queue priorities and watermarks can be tuned for latency or throughput. TCSTATUS and related registers expose starvation or FIFO conditions. Misconfiguration can stall channels even when CC is healthy. Correct mapping of channels to queues balances peripheral and memory traffic.
evidence: EDMA3 UG: TC registers (e.g., TCSTATUS) and queue configuration
dependencies: EDMA3 CC configured; Queue to channel mapping; Memory bandwidth
Remarks: Profile queue depths under load and adjust priorities to prevent head-of-line blocking.

description: QDMA channels trigger transfers via CPU writes to a trigger word in the PaRAM set. They are well-suited for small or latency-sensitive copies without event wiring. CIPR/CIER/CICR provide per-QDMA interrupt control. Care is required to avoid overwriting active PaRAM entries. QDMA can coexist with event-driven channels to optimize system performance.
evidence: EDMA3 UG: QCHMAP, CIPR/CIER/CICR and QDMA trigger mechanism
dependencies: EDMA3 CC; Proper PaRAM reservation; INTMUX for QDMA interrupts
Remarks: Use dedicated PaRAM slots for QDMA and guard with software semaphores in RTOS environments.

description: The CC reports missed events and internal errors to help diagnose transfer issues. EMR/EMRH indicate missed events, and CCERR/CCERRCLR report controller-level faults. Software should log and clear these conditions before re-enabling channels. Persistent errors often indicate addressability or permission problems. System health monitoring should periodically sample these registers.
evidence: EDMA3 UG: EMR/EMRH, CCERR/CCERRCLR, SECR/SECRH semantics
dependencies: EDMA3 CC active; Interrupt mapping for error events
Remarks: Include error ISR hooks and counters to enable rapid field diagnostics.

description: The DDR2 controller initializes external SDRAM with device-specific timings. Software programs SDCR, SDTIMR1/2, and SDRCR, then enables refresh and verifies PHY/VTP readiness. Correct parameters depend on the memory data sheet and clock rates. A basic memory test should follow initialization to catch routing or timing issues. The controller is essential for large working sets and code storage.
evidence: SPRUF85: Registers SDCR, SDTIMR1/2, SDRCR; PHY/VTP control; Initialization section
dependencies: Main PLL frequency; PSC enable; Board DDR routing; Power sequencing
Remarks: Generate timings from SPD/data sheet and lock them in configuration headers per board variant.

description: The CPDMA engine moves Ethernet frames between host memory and MAC ports using descriptor rings. Software initializes TX/RX descriptor queues in CPPI RAM and programs TX_HDP/RX_HDP and TX_CP/RX_CP pointers. TX/RX_CONTROL enable data flow after a SOFT_RESET clears the engine. Completion processing reclaims descriptors and can signal via interrupts. Proper buffer alignment and ring sizing are critical for throughput.
evidence: SPRUF97: CPDMA registers TX_CONTROL, RX_CONTROL, TX_HDP[n], RX_HDP[n], TX_CP[n], RX_CP[n], SOFT_RESET
dependencies: PSC enable; EMAC slivers; ALE; SGMII/MDIO; Pinmux
Remarks: Adopt zero-copy where possible and segregate RX rings by priority or packet size.

description: Each MAC Sliver" provides per-port control and statistics for link operation. MAC_CONTROL configures speed
evidence: duplex
dependencies: while RX_MAXLEN sets frame acceptance. Hardware MAC address registers define the host port identity. Status registers expose errors and link state to software. Proper sliver configuration aligns with PHY settings to avoid mismatches."
Driver required: SPRUF97: CPGMAC Sliver registers MAC_CONTROL, MAC_STATUS, RX_MAXLEN, MAC_ADDR_LO/HI
Remarks: Explicit
column10: CPDMA enabled; PHY negotiated parameters; Pinmux
column12: Keep MAC and PHY configurations synchronized and lock speed/duplex when autoneg issues are observed.

description: The ALE provides L2 switching functions including address learning, filtering, and VLAN control. Software enables ALE, clears the table, and inserts unicast or multicast entries as needed. Port control registers set forwarding state and ingress/egress rules. Proper ALE configuration is required even in single-port host-only use to pass traffic. Table management policies impact performance and security.
evidence: SPRUF97: ALE_CONTROL, ALE_TABLE entry format, ALE_PORTCTL[n]
dependencies: CPDMA and Slivers initialized; System MAC addresses; VLAN plan
Remarks: Initialize ALE early and consider static entries for control traffic and known peers.

description: The MDIO block manages external PHYs and reports link status. CONTROL configures the clock divider and enables the interface, while ALIVE/LINK reflect PHY presence and link state. USERACCESS registers issue read and write frames to PHY registers. Reliable MDIO timing is required for SGMII or copper PHY bring-up. Software should poll or interrupt on link changes.
evidence: SPRUF97: MDIO CONTROL, ALIVE, LINK, USERACCESS0/1, USERPHYSEL0/1
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; External PHYs powered
Remarks: Centralize PHY access and debounce link state to avoid flapping in upper layers.

description: SGMII blocks configure the serial link between MAC and external PHY or backplane. CONTROL and STATUS registers manage autonegotiation and link parameters. Mismatched settings can prevent link or cause unstable operation. Initialization must follow power and reference clock stabilization. Software should verify link before enabling traffic on associated slivers.
evidence: SPRUF97: SGMII_CONTROL, SGMII_STATUS per port
dependencies: EMAC slivers; MDIO-managed PHYs; Stable reference clocks
Remarks: Prefer autonegotiation when interoperating with standard PHYs; force mode only for lab debug.

description: Ethernet data paths use CPPI-style descriptors organized in linked rings. The host writes descriptor base addresses to HDP registers and reclaims via CP registers on completion. Descriptors contain buffer pointers, lengths, and flags consumed by CPDMA. Correct ownership handoff is necessary to prevent descriptor leaks. Cache coherency policies must align with descriptor and buffer placement.
evidence: SPRUF97: CPDMA descriptor model and HDP/CP usage
dependencies: CPDMA enabled; Coherent memory plan; Cache control
Remarks: Place descriptor rings in non-cached or write-back-managed regions and align to hardware requirements.

description: The PCI module implements 32-bit conventional PCI with master and target capabilities. Software configures local configuration space including BARs and command/status, and maps inbound/outbound address windows. Integration with EDMA enables high-throughput data movement between PCI and memory. Enumeration requires correct reset and clocking per PCI specifications. Interrupts and message mechanisms signal events to the DSP and host.
evidence: SPRUF86: PCI local configuration registers, BARs, command/status; window mapping registers
dependencies: PSC enable; PLL frequency for PCI; Optional EDMA; Pinmux
Remarks: Validate BAR sizing and address decoding against host BIOS/OS expectations before enabling bus mastering.

description: UHPI provides a parallel host interface for external processors to access DSP memory. Key registers include HPIA for addressing, HPID for data, and HPIC for control and interrupts. Proper ownership and endian configuration are required before transactions. UHPI can accelerate bulk transfers during bootstrapping or diagnostics. Interrupts notify the DSP of host-initiated events.
evidence: SPRUF87: UHPI registers HPIA, HPID, HPIC and status
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; Interrupt mapping; Endianness settings
Remarks: Define a clear host/DSP protocol and guard shared regions with semaphores or mailboxes.

description: McASP supports TDM/I2S/DIT modes with independent RX/TX clocking and serializers. Initialization programs PFUNC/PDIR, clock controls ACLKx/AHCLKx, formats XFMT/RFMT, time slots XTDM/RTDM, and serializer SRCTLn roles. The GBLCTL enable sequence must strictly follow the documented order. Optional FIFO/DMA integration offloads data movement. Misordered bring-up leads to stuck state machines or underruns.
evidence: SPRUF91: Registers PFUNC, PDIR, GBLCTL, ACLKXCTL/AHCLKXCTL, XFMT/RFMT, XTDM/RTDM, SRCTLn
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; EDMA channels for RX/TX
Remarks: Encapsulate the exact GBLCTL step sequence and assert error recovery paths for R/XSTAT faults.

description: The SPI module implements full-duplex serial transfers with programmable formats. SPIGCR0/1 control reset and master/slave, while SPIFMTn select word length, polarity, phase, and baud. Data is written via SPIDATx and read via SPIBUF with status in SPIFLG. Interrupts or EDMA can service TX/RX events. Proper chip-select handling and delays ensure device compatibility.
evidence: SPRUF92: SPIGCR0, SPIGCR1, SPIPC0, SPIFMT0–3, SPIDAT0/1, SPIBUF, SPIFLG, SPIDELAY
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; Optional EDMA; External device timing
Remarks: Standardize CPOL/CPHA profiles and validate maximum SCLK against attached devices.

description: The UART provides 16550-style serial communication with enhanced features and FIFOs. Configuration uses MDR1 to select mode, DLL/DLH for baud, and LCR for frame format. IIR/FCR manage FIFO levels and interrupt identification, while LSR reflects status. Correct disable-program-enable sequence avoids glitches. Interrupt-driven operation reduces CPU load for RX/TX.
evidence: SPRUF93: THR/RBR, IER, IIR/FCR, LCR, MCR, LSR, DLL/DLH, EFR, MDR1
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; Interrupt mapping
Remarks: Use FIFOs with sane watermarks and ensure accurate input clock in baud calculations.

description: The I2C module supports master and slave transfers with programmable clocking. ICPSC and ICCLKL/H derive SCL, while ICSAR sets the target address, and ICMDR controls start/stop and direction. Data moves through ICDXR/ICDRR, with status in ICSTR and interrupts via ICIMR/ICIVR. Bus recovery and NACK handling are essential for robustness. Timing must respect standard or fast mode limits.
evidence: SPRUF94: ICOAR, ICSAR, ICPSC, ICCLKL/ICCLKH, ICMDR, ICSTR, ICDXR, ICDRR, ICIMR, ICIVR
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; Pull-ups present; Interrupt mapping
Remarks: Implement timeouts and a clock-stretch recovery to handle stuck bus scenarios.

description: GPIO provides up to 32 general-purpose inputs/outputs with synchronized inputs and edge detection. Direction is set per bit in DIR01, and outputs are driven via SET_DATA01/CLR_DATA01. Edge detection is configured through SET_RIS_TRIG01 and SET_FAL_TRIG01 with latched status in INSTAT01. Banked interrupts can be routed to CPU via INTMUX. Proper debounce and masking avoid spurious transitions.
evidence: SPRUF95: Registers PID, BINTEN, DIR01, SET_DATA01, CLR_DATA01, IN_DATA01, SET_RIS_TRIG01, SET_FAL_TRIG01, INSTAT01
dependencies: PSC enable; Pinmux; INTMUX for bank interrupts
Remarks: Use write-1-to-set/clear accessors and clear all latched sources before exiting the ISR.

description: Timer64 can operate as one 64-bit timer or two independent 32-bit timers. TGCR sets mode and reset, while TCR configures start/stop and interrupt generation. PRD12/PRD34 define periods and TIM12/TIM34 hold counters. Timers can generate periodic interrupts for scheduling or capture events. Proper PSC enable and ISR mapping are required for reliable operation.
evidence: Timer64 UG: TGCR, TCR, PRD12/34, TIM12/34 registers
dependencies: PSC enable; INTMUX mapping; Clock source selection
Remarks: Calibrate timer frequency against CPU clock and guard against missed ticks in long ISRs.

description: L1P/L1D and L2 caches improve performance when configured with appropriate sizes and attributes. MAR registers control cacheability of external address ranges, while L1/L2 control registers manage enables and write policies. Incorrect attributes can cause coherency issues with DMA engines. Initialization should align cache and EDMA buffer placement. Profiling guides decisions on cache partitioning and locking.
evidence: SPRU732: MARx registers; L1/L2 cache control and configuration sections
dependencies: EDMA usage; External memory map; CPDMA descriptors
Remarks: Define a coherent memory plan, including carve-outs for DMA, before enabling caches.

description: Boot ROM selects and executes a boot mode based on strap pins and configuration registers. Software can read boot status in device configuration to determine boot source and success. Some modes hand off control with peripherals partially configured. Understanding boot flow is essential for secondary bootloaders. Post-boot code should sanitize clocks, PSC, and critical modules.
evidence: SPRS371: Device Configuration and Boot sections; Device Config registers
dependencies: Board boot straps; Device Config registers accessible
Remarks: Log boot mode and status early and reinitialize critical clocks and power states deterministically.

description: The device configuration block exposes silicon device ID and revision registers. Software can read these to apply errata workarounds or feature gating. This enables safer deployments across mask revisions. Access is read-only and has no side effects. Logging the ID at startup aids field diagnostics.
evidence: Device Configuration module: Device ID/Revision registers (see SPRS371)
dependencies: NONE
Remarks: Gate workarounds and feature enables on exact revision checks to avoid unnecessary constraints.
